is a widely used unit of [local government in the United States](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_States "Local government in the United States") that is subordinate to a [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)"), most often in the northern and midwestern parts of the country. The term **town** is used in [New England](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town"), [New York](/wiki/Political_subdivisions_of_New_York_State#Town "Political subdivisions of New York State"), as well as [Wisconsin](/wiki/Political_subdivisions_of_Wisconsin#Town "Political subdivisions of Wisconsin") to refer to the equivalent of the civil township in these states; [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") uses "town" officially but often uses it and "township" interchangeably. Specific responsibilities and the degree of [autonomy](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/autonomy "wiktionary:autonomy") vary in each [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state"). Civil townships are distinct from [survey townships](/wiki/Survey_township "Survey township"), but in states that have both, the boundaries often coincide, especially in Indiana, Ohio, and Illinois, and may completely geographically subdivide a county. The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") classifies civil townships as [minor civil divisions](/wiki/Minor_civil_division "Minor civil division"). Currently, there are 20 states with civil townships, including Indiana, Ohio, Illinois, Michigan, Iowa, Minnesota, Wisconsin, Missouri, Kansas, Nebraska, South Dakota, North Dakota, Pennsylvania, and West Virginia (in certain areas).

A **civil township** is a widely used unit of [local government in the United States](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_States "Local government in the United States") that is subordinate to a [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)"), most often in the northern and midwestern parts of the country. The term **town** is used in [New England](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town"), [New York](/wiki/Political_subdivisions_of_New_York_State#Town "Political subdivisions of New York State"), as well as [Wisconsin](/wiki/Political_subdivisions_of_Wisconsin#Town "Political subdivisions of Wisconsin") to refer to the equivalent of the civil township in these states; [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") uses "town" officially but often uses it and "township" interchangeably. Specific responsibilities and the degree of [autonomy](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/autonomy "wiktionary:autonomy") vary in each [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state"). Civil townships are distinct from [survey townships](/wiki/Survey_township "Survey township"), but in states that have both, the boundaries often coincide, especially in Indiana, Ohio, and Illinois, and may completely geographically subdivide a county. The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") classifies civil townships as [minor civil divisions](/wiki/Minor_civil_division "Minor civil division"). Currently, there are 20 states with civil townships, including Indiana, Ohio, Illinois, Michigan, Iowa, Minnesota, Wisconsin, Missouri, Kansas, Nebraska, South Dakota, North Dakota, Pennsylvania, and West Virginia (in certain areas).

Township functions are generally overseen by a governing board (the name varies from state to state) and a clerk, trustee, or mayor (in [New Jersey](/wiki/Township_(New_Jersey) "Township (New Jersey)") and the [metro townships](/wiki/Metro_township "Metro township") of [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah")). Township officers frequently include [justice of the peace](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace "Justice of the peace"), [road commissioner](/wiki/Numbered_highways_in_the_United_States "Numbered highways in the United States"), [assessor](/wiki/Assessor_(property) "Assessor (property)"), and [constable](/wiki/Constable "Constable"), in addition to [surveyor](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying"). In the 20th century, many townships also added a township [administrator](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_Government "Administrator of the Government") or [supervisor](/wiki/Supervisor "Supervisor") to the officers as an [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") for the board. In some cases, townships run local [libraries](/wiki/Library "Library"), [senior citizen](/wiki/Old_age "Old age") services, youth services, [disabled](/wiki/Disability "Disability") citizen services, and [cemetery](/wiki/Cemetery "Cemetery") services, besides emergency assistance. In some states, a township and a municipality that is coterminous with that township may wholly or partially [consolidate](/wiki/Coterminous_municipality "Coterminous municipality") their operations.

## Midwestern and central

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Civil_township&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Midwestern and central")]

See also: [Indiana township trustee](/wiki/Indiana_township_trustee "Indiana township trustee"), [Political subdivisions of Wisconsin](/wiki/Political_subdivisions_of_Wisconsin "Political subdivisions of Wisconsin"), and [Charter township](/wiki/Charter_township "Charter township")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Madison_Township_Hall.jpg/250px-Madison_Township_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madison_Township_Hall.jpg)

Madison Township Hall in [Madison Township](/wiki/Madison_Township,_Richland_County,_Ohio "Madison Township, Richland County, Ohio"), [Richland County](/wiki/Richland_County,_Ohio "Richland County, Ohio"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio")

Depending on the state, the township government has varying degrees of authority.

In the [Upper Midwestern](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest") states near the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"), civil townships (known in [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan") as general law townships[[1]](#cite_note-1) and in Wisconsin as towns), are often, but not always, overlaid on survey townships. The degree to which these townships are functioning governmental entities varies from state to state and in some cases even within a state. For example, townships in the northern part of [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") are active in providing public services—such as road maintenance, after-school care, and senior services—whereas townships in southern Illinois frequently delegate these services to the county. Most townships in Illinois also provide services such as snow removal, senior transportation, and emergency services to households residing in unincorporated parts of the county. The townships in Illinois each have a township board, whose board members were formerly called [township trustees](/wiki/Township_trustee "Township trustee"), and a single township supervisor. In contrast, civil townships in [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") are operated in a relatively consistent manner statewide and tend to be well organized, with each served by a single [township trustee](/wiki/Indiana_township_trustee "Indiana township trustee") and a three-member board.

Civil townships in these states are generally not [incorporated](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation"), and nearby cities may [annex](/wiki/Annexation "Annexation") land in adjoining townships with relative ease. In Michigan, however, general law townships are corporate entities (e.g. they can be the subject of lawsuits),[[2]](#cite_note-2) and some can become reformulated as [charter townships](/wiki/Charter_township "Charter township"), a status intended to protect against annexation from nearby municipalities and which grants the township some [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") powers similar to cities. In [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), civil townships are known as "[towns](/wiki/Political_subdivisions_of_Wisconsin#Town "Political subdivisions of Wisconsin")" rather than townships, but they function essentially the same as in neighboring states. In [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), state statute refers to such entities as towns yet requires them to have a name in the form "*Name* Township".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In both documents and conversation, "town" and "township" are used interchangeably. Minnesota townships can be either [Non-Urban](/w/index.php?title=Non-urban_township&action=edit&redlink=1 "Non-urban township (page does not exist)") or [Urban](/wiki/Urban_township "Urban township") (giving the township government greater power), but this is not reflected in the township's name.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Similarly, in [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), some townships qualify for [urban township](/wiki/Urban_township_(Ohio) "Urban township (Ohio)") status, affording limited home rule government. A city or village is overlaid onto a township unless it withdraws by establishing a [paper township](/wiki/Paper_township "Paper township"). Where the paper township does not extend to the city limits, property owners pay taxes for both the township and municipality,[[4]](#cite_note-MedinaCo_2005-4) though these overlaps are sometimes overlooked by mistake.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Ten other states also allow townships and municipalities to overlap.[[6]](#cite_note-Census_townships-6)

In [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"), some civil townships provide services such as road maintenance and fire protection services not provided by the county.

## Northeastern states

### New England and New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Civil_township&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: New England and New York")]

In [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") and [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), states are generally subdivided into towns and cities, which are municipalities that provide most local services. With the exception of a few remote areas of [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") and about half of [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), all of New England lies within the borders of an incorporated municipality. New England has counties, though in southern New England, they are strictly used as dividing lines for judicial systems and statistical purposes, while in northern New England, they often handle other limited functions, such as law enforcement, education and some public facilities in addition to judicial systems. New England also has [cities](/wiki/City "City"), most of which are towns whose residents have voted to replace the town meeting form of government with a city form. Maine has a third type of township called a [plantation](/wiki/Plantation_(Maine) "Plantation (Maine)"), which previously existed in other New England states, that has more limited self-governance than other New England towns.

In portions of Maine, New Hampshire, and Vermont, county subdivisions that are not incorporated are occasionally called townships, or by other terms such as "[gore](/wiki/Gore_(surveying) "Gore (surveying)")", "grant", "location", or "purchase".

The [State of New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") is subdivided into counties, which are subdivided into cities and towns—except for [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), whose five coextensive counties for state government purposes are municipal boroughs for city government purposes ([Bronx County/The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), [Kings County/Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [New York County/Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), [Queens County/Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), and [Richmond County/Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island")). As in New England, the term "town" equates to "township" in most other states. Additionally, New Yorkers colloquially use the term "township" to mean "town". Townships and [hamlets](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York#Hamlet "Administrative divisions of New York") are unincorporated areas within a town or towns. Because towns are administrative divisions of a county, town boundaries cannot cross county lines. In addition to administrative subdivisions, New York State also has [cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_York "List of cities in New York"). Cities in New York are fully autonomous municipal corporations and, thus, are able to cross county lines and whose governments fully independent of county control.

Finally, New York and Vermont also have villages, which are smaller communities lying within the boundaries of a town that provide additional government services not provided by their parent town, such as sewage, fire, law enforcement, garbage collection, public facilities, water and building code enforcement. In Vermont, most current cities were actually former villages that broke off from their parent town. Connecticut has [boroughs](/wiki/Borough_(Connecticut) "Borough (Connecticut)") and non-consolidated cities, although these communities are not as autonomous as villages in New York and Vermont, and today there are only eight non-consolidated boroughs (plus the community of [Groton Long Point](/wiki/Groton_Long_Point "Groton Long Point")) and one non-consolidated city.

### Pennsylvania and New Jersey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Civil_township&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Pennsylvania and New Jersey")]

A [Pennsylvania township](/wiki/Township_(Pennsylvania) "Township (Pennsylvania)") is a unit of local government, responsible for services such as police departments, local road and street [maintenance](/wiki/Maintenance,_repair_and_operations "Maintenance, repair and operations"). It acts the same as a city or [borough](/wiki/Borough "Borough"). Townships were established based on convenient geographical boundaries and vary in size from six to fifty-two square miles (10–135 km2).

A [New Jersey township](/wiki/Township_(New_Jersey) "Township (New Jersey)") is similar, in that it is a form of municipal government equal in status to a [village](/wiki/Village_(New_Jersey) "Village (New Jersey)"), [town](/wiki/Town_(New_Jersey) "Town (New Jersey)"), [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)"), or [city](/wiki/City_(New_Jersey) "City (New Jersey)"), and provides similar services to a Pennsylvania township, and varies in size from one-tenth ([Shrewsbury Twp.](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Township,_New_Jersey "Shrewsbury Township, New Jersey")) to one hundred ([Jackson Twp.](/wiki/Jackson_Township,_New_Jersey "Jackson Township, New Jersey")) square miles.

### Arkansas and North Carolina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Civil_township&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Arkansas and North Carolina")]

[Arkansas townships](/wiki/List_of_Arkansas_townships "List of Arkansas townships") are the divisions of a county. Each township includes unincorporated areas; some may have incorporated cities or towns within part of their boundaries. Arkansas townships have limited purposes in modern times. However, the [U.S. census](/wiki/United_States_census "United States census") does list Arkansas population based on townships (sometimes referred to as "county subdivisions" or "minor civil divisions"). Townships are also of value for historical purposes in terms of genealogical research. Each town or city is within one or more townships in an Arkansas county based on census maps and publications.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina") is no exception to that rule, but it does have townships as minor geographical subdivisions of counties, including both unincorporated territory and also land within the bounds of incorporated cities and towns (as well as the [extraterritorial jurisdiction](/wiki/Extraterritorial_jurisdiction "Extraterritorial jurisdiction") of municipalities). Every county is divided into townships as mandated since the [North Carolina Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_North_Carolina "Constitution of North Carolina") of 1868. Some urbanized counties such as [Mecklenburg County](/wiki/Mecklenburg_County,_North_Carolina "Mecklenburg County, North Carolina") ([Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina")) now number their townships (e.g. "Township 12") rather than using names. Townships all over the state used to have some official organization and duties but now are only considered [ceremonial](/wiki/Ceremonial "Ceremonial") divisions of each county. Township names are still used quite extensively at the county government level in North Carolina as a way of determining and dividing up areas for administrative purposes; primarily for collecting county taxes, determining fire districts (e.g. [Lebanon Township](/wiki/Lebanon_Township,_Durham_County,_North_Carolina "Lebanon Township, Durham County, North Carolina") in [Durham County](/wiki/Durham_County,_North_Carolina#Townships "Durham County, North Carolina") gives its name to the [Lebanon Volunteer Fire Department](http://lebanonvfd.org/)), for real estate purposes such as categorizing land deeds, [land surveys](/wiki/Cadastre "Cadastre") and other real estate documents, and for voter registration purposes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In most areas of North Carolina that are outside any municipal limit (outside cities or towns), townships are used to determine voter polling places, and in most instances county election boards divide up their voter precincts by township.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, there is no government per se at the township level in North Carolina, and there are no elected or appointed offices associated with townships.

### Utah

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Civil_township&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Utah")]

Between 2015 and 2024, [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") had a form of civil township called a [metro township](/wiki/Metro_township "Metro township"). Each metro township had a mayor,[[8]](#cite_note-8) council, and a budget, and could not be annexed without its permission.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Its powers of taxation were limited, and it had to contract with other municipalities or municipal shared-service districts[[10]](#cite_note-10) for most municipal services like police.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In May 2024 the five metro townships, all located in [Salt Lake County](/wiki/Salt_Lake_County "Salt Lake County"), were incorporated as cities or towns as a result of Utah's House Bill 35.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## States which once had but no longer have civil townships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Civil_township&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: States which once had but no longer have civil townships")]

* California had townships at statehood. County boards of supervisors had the authority to create, consolidate, or abolish townships as late as the 1920s.[[14]](#cite_note-14) (See [Category:Former townships in California](/wiki/Category:Former_townships_in_California "Category:Former townships in California").) A system of township [assessors](/wiki/Tax_assessment "Tax assessment") was established in some counties,[[15]](#cite_note-15) but under the [1879 Constitution](/wiki/California_Constitutional_Conventions#Sacramento_Convention_of_1878–79 "California Constitutional Conventions"), townships were limited to judicial and law enforcement districts rather than administrative ones.[[16]](#cite_note-16) As of 1967, every county was divided into townships for electoral purposes,[[17]](#cite_note-17) but this was no longer the case by 1977.[[18]](#cite_note-18) [San Joaquin County](/wiki/San_Joaquin_County,_California "San Joaquin County, California")'s legal code continues to define that county's townships, although it does not delegate any powers or responsibilities to them.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* Iowa had 1,608 townships in 1942. By 1952, their responsibilities and functions had essentially been absorbed by their counties, were considered "vestigial", and were no longer counted.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Townships exist in Iowa, but are counted as administrative divisions of their counties and are not counted as having separate governments by the United States Census Bureau.
* [Oklahoma](/wiki/List_of_Oklahoma_townships "List of Oklahoma townships") had civil townships statewide upon statehood. On August 5, 1913, Oklahoma voters passed the Oklahoma Township Amendment, also known as State Question 58. This allowed the creation or abolishment of townships on a county by county basis.[[21]](#cite_note-21) By the mid-1930s, all counties had voted to abolish them.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Their former names and boundaries were used for United States census counting purposes and census documentation through 1960, after which Census Counting Divisions (CCDs) were used.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* South Carolina had 2 townships in 1952, those of [Folly Island](/wiki/Folly_Island "Folly Island") and [Sullivan's Island](/wiki/Sullivan%27s_Island,_South_Carolina "Sullivan's Island, South Carolina").[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* The 1870 Constitution of Virginia required the division of counties into townships, but an amendment in 1874 converted those townships into magisterial districts.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* Washington had 70 townships in 1952, in only Spokane and Whatcom counties, for administration of local roads.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Civil_township&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Canada")]

Main article: [Township (Canada)](/wiki/Township_(Canada) "Township (Canada)")

The provinces of [Eastern Canada](/wiki/Eastern_Canada "Eastern Canada") have, or have had, divisions that are analogous to the townships of the United States.

[Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") continue to have townships that subdivide their respective counties.

Local government was introduced into [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") (which at the time also included the [maritime provinces](/wiki/Maritime_provinces "Maritime provinces") of [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") and [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island")) in 1749 in the form of townships controlled by a [court of quarter sessions](/wiki/Court_of_quarter_sessions "Court of quarter sessions"), a system that had characterized local government in Britain and Virginia. This court had both administrative and judicial functions, and took over most responsibilities of local government, including the appointment of necessary officers. A form of direct democracy was introduced into the townships by settlers from New England in 1760 with the help of a provincial statute. However, the statute was [disallowed](/wiki/Disallowance_and_reservation "Disallowance and reservation") by the King in 1761, and direct democracy was replaced in the townships that had adopted it by the quarter sessions. Acts in 1855 and 1856 provided for the incorporation of self-governing townships, but only [Yarmouth](/wiki/Yarmouth,_Nova_Scotia "Yarmouth, Nova Scotia") partook in incorporating, and abandoned the prospect after three years. These acts were repealed in 1879, and townships were replaced in the third quarter of the 19th century by self-governing municipalities.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
